papers.title: Short-duration lensing events: I. wide-orbit planets? free-floating low-mass objects? or high-velocity stars?

abstract.text: Short duration lensing events tend to be generated by low-mass lenses or by lenses with high transverse velocities. Furthermore, for any given lens mass and speed, events of short duration are preferentially caused by nearby lenses (mesolenses) that can be studied in detail, or else by lenses so close to the source star that finite-source-size effects may be detected, yielding information about both the Einstein ring radius and the surface of the lensed star. Planets causing short-duration events may be in orbits with any orientation, and may have semimajor axes smaller than an AU, or they may reach the outer limits of their planetary systems, in the region corresponding to the Solar System's Oort Cloud. They can have masses larger than Jupiter's or smaller than Pluto's. Lensing therefore has a unique potential to expand our understanding of planetary systems. A particular advantage of lensing is that it can provide precision measurements of system parameters, including the masses of and projected separation between star and planet. We demonstrate how the parameters can be extracted and show that a great deal can be learned. For example, it is remarkable that the gravitational mass of nearby free-floating planet-mass lenses can be measured by complementing observations of a photometric event with deep images that detect the planet itself. A fraction of short events may be caused by high-velocity stars located within a kpc. Many high-velocity lenses are likely to be neutron stars that received large natal kicks. Other high-speed stars may be members of the halo population. Still others may be hypervelocity stars that have been ejected from the Galactic Center, or runaway stars escaped from close binaries, possibly including the progenitor binaries of Type Ia supernovae.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] Short-duration gravitational lensing events (with Einstein-diameter crossing times on the order of days) constrain lens candidates to a narrow set of physical models: low-mass planets, brown dwarfs, or high-velocity stars (e.g., neutron stars, hypervelocity/runaway white dwarfs). Near-source or nearby lenses enable precise mass and velocity measurements via photometric, astrometric, and finite-source-size effects; for free-floating planets, direct detection or deep imaging can yield gravitational mass measurements. Lensing offers unique access to planetary system parameters, including mass ratios and orbital separations, and provides a pathway to discover and characterize high-velocity stellar remnants, including potential progenitors of Type Ia supernovae.

source_fields.title: Short-duration lensing events: I. wide-orbit planets? free-floating low-mass objects? or high-velocity stars?

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces the novel insight that short Einstein-diameter crossing times (on the order of days) strongly constrain lensing events to a narrow range of physical models—such as planets, brown dwarfs, or high-velocity stars—enabling precise mass measurements and offering a powerful pathway to discover and study elusive objects like neutron stars and hypervelocity stars. Focus on Short Events: Short events in microlensing are highly valuable for identifying nearby, low-mass objects like free-floating planets and high-velocity stars, enabling precise gravitational mass measurements and providing key insights into planetary populations and stellar remnants without requiring sophisticated astrometry. Planets: Bound or Free-Floating?: This section demonstrates that free-floating planets can be identified through microlensing events by combining light curve analysis with proper motion and Einstein angle constraints, enabling distance, mass, and orbital inference while distinguishing them from bound planetary systems. Short-duration Events: What Can the Lenses Be?: This section outlines the physical parameters and observational constraints for microlensing events, showing how the Einstein angle, crossing time, and lens mass can be derived from light curve data and astrophysical measurements, with blending and detection sensitivity playing key roles in interpreting short-duration events. Overview of Model Tests: Breaking the Degeneracy: The method proposes using high-cadence photometric observations of short-duration microlensing events to constrain lens mass, detect finite-source-size effects, and identify signatures of companions or high-velocity stellar remnants by analyzing light curve shapes and potential astrometric shifts.

abstract: A fraction of short events may be caused by high-velocity stars located within a kpc. Many high-velocity lenses are likely to be neutron stars that received large natal kicks.
Other high-speed stars may be members of the halo population.

Still others may be hypervelocity stars that have been ejected from the Galactic Center, or runaway stars escaped from close binaries, possibly including the progenitor binaries of Type Ia supernovae.
title: or high-velocity stars?

catalog_assessment.evidence: Theoretical study of gravitational lensing events; discusses high-velocity stars as potential lens candidates but presents no observational catalog or object-level data

papers.title: OB Stars & Stellar Bowshocks in Cygnus-X: A Novel Laboratory Estimating Stellar Mass Loss Rates
papers.author_names: Henry A. Kobulnicky Ian J. Gilbert Daniel C. Kiminki

abstract.text: We use mid-IR images from the Spitzer Cygnus~X Legacy Survey to search for stellar bowshocks, a signature of early type "runaway" stars with high space velocities. We identify ten arc-shaped nebulae containing centrally located stars as candidate bowshocks. New spectroscopic observations of five stars show that all are late O to early B dwarfs. Our morphologically selected sample of bowshock candidates encompasses diverse physical phenomena. Three of the stars appear to be pre-main-sequence objects on the basis of rising SEDs in the mid-IR, and their nebulae may be photon-dominated regions (PDRs). Four objects have ambiguous classification. These may be partial dust shells or bubbles. We conclude that three of the objects are probable bowshocks, based on their morphological similarity to analytic prescriptions. Their nebular morphologies reveal no systematic pattern of orientations that might indicate either a population of stars ejected from or large-scale hydrodynamic outflows from Cyg OB2. The fraction of runaways among OB stars near Cyg OB2 identified either by radial velocity or bowshock techniques is ~0.5%, much smaller than the 8% estimated among field OB stars. We also obtained a heliocentric radial velocity for the previously known bowshock star, BD+43\\\\degr3654, of -66.2+/-9.4 km/s, solidifying its runaway status and implying a space velocity of 77+/-10 km/s. We use the principles of momentum-driven bowshocks to arrive at a novel method for estimating stellar mass loss rates. Derived mass loss rates range between 10^-7 and few x10^-6 solar masses/yr for the three O5V -- ~B2V stars identified as generating bowshocks. These values are at the upper range of, but broadly consistent with, estimates from other methods. (Abridged)

triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies early-type 'runaway' stars with high space velocities using bowshock signatures, includes velocity measurements and runaway fraction analysis

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section describes the retrieval and analysis of Spitzer and WIRO data to identify and characterize bowshock candidates in the Cygnus-X complex, using infrared imaging and optical spectroscopy to link stellar sources with symmetric arc-like structures. Analysis: The analysis of bowshock candidates in Cygnus-X reveals no clear evidence of runaways from Cyg OB2 or a large-scale outflow, suggesting the bowshocks are not driven by central massive stars or a collective wind from the association. Conclusion: This study identifies ten candidate bowshock nebulae around Cyg OB2, confirms several runaway stars through radial velocities and proper motions, identifies young stellar objects with circumstellar material, and proposes a novel method for estimating mass loss rates in runaway OB stars using bowshock physics. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to identify runaway O stars by combining high-precision proper motions with dynamical modeling of binary interactions, addressing the problem of distinguishing between binary-supernova and dynamical ejection scenarios in runaway star formation. Bowshocks and Laboratories: This section presents the theoretical and observational framework for estimating the relative motion between massive stars and the ambient interstellar medium using bowshock standoff distances and stellar wind properties.

abstract: We use mid-IR images from the Spitzer Cygnus~X Legacy Survey to search for stellar bowshocks, a signature of early type "runaway" stars with high space velocities.
We identify ten arc-shaped nebulae containing centrally located stars as candidate bowshocks. New spectroscopic observations of five stars show that all are late O to early B dwarfs.
Three of the stars appear to be pre-main-sequence objects on the basis of rising SEDs in the mid-IR, and their nebulae may be photon-dominated regions (PDRs).
The fraction of runaways among OB stars near Cyg OB2 identified either by radial velocity or bowshock techniques is ~0.5%, much smaller than the 8% estimated among field OB stars.
Derived mass loss rates range between 10^-7 and few x10^-6 solar masses/yr for the three O5V -- ~B2V stars identified as generating bowshocks.
title: OB Stars & Stellar Bowshocks in Cygnus-X: A Novel Laboratory Estimating Stellar Mass Loss Rates
